stem: Which possible feature of a group risk plan in a country overlaps with the concepts of multi-national pooling and may make it difficult to pool?
explanation: Local profit sharing overlaps with multi-national pooling because both involve returning surplus/profit to the policyholder. If a local plan already has its own profit-sharing arrangement, integrating it into a multi-national pool creates a conflict over how surpluses are distributed.

stem: Which main area of the group risk market normally generates the most sales?
explanation: Lump-sum death benefits (group life assurance) are the most widely purchased group risk product in the UK market. They are simpler to understand, relatively inexpensive, and are the most common employee benefit offered by employers.

stem: When a higher-rate taxpayer invests £50 per month into a Save As You Earn (SAYE) Plan, what rate of tax, if any, will be levied on any cash bonus payable on maturity?
explanation: SAYE schemes are tax-advantaged savings plans linked to share option schemes. The cash bonus payable on maturity of the savings contract is completely tax-free, regardless of the saver's tax rate.

stem: Apart from a death benefit lump sum and a dependant's pension, what other form of benefit would usually be provided, if any, by a single employer's trust-based money purchase pension scheme on death?
options: Return of both employee's and employer's contributions.
explanation: In a trust-based money purchase (defined contribution) pension scheme, on death the fund value is typically used to provide a lump sum and/or dependant's pension. In addition, a return of the employee's own contributions is usually provided as a separate benefit. Employer contributions form part of the fund rather than being returned separately.

stem: Bereavement Support Payment can now be claimed by
options: partners that receive or are entitled to child benefits or are pregnant.
explanation: Bereavement Support Payment replaced the previous Bereavement Allowance and is available to spouses and civil partners who are either receiving or entitled to Child Benefit, or who are pregnant. It is not available to all partners (cohabiting partners are excluded) nor restricted to only spouses or only civil partners.

stem: John is an employee. At what level will his employer start making National Insurance contributions?
explanation: Employers pay Class 1 secondary National Insurance contributions on earnings above the secondary threshold. The primary threshold is the point at which employees start paying NICs, not employers. The lower earnings limit and upper earnings limit serve different purposes.

stem: For what maximum period is Statutory Sick Pay payable?
explanation: Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) is payable by the employer for a maximum of 28 weeks. After SSP is exhausted, the employee may be able to claim Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) from the state.

stem: What type of trust is normally used to establish a group death-in-service scheme?
explanation: A discretionary trust is normally used for group death-in-service schemes because it gives the trustees discretion over who receives the benefits. This flexibility means the lump sum death benefit can be paid to the most appropriate beneficiary and, crucially, falls outside the deceased's estate for inheritance tax purposes.

stem: A redundancy payment will be subject to tax if it exceeds what threshold?
explanation: The first £30,000 of a genuine redundancy payment is exempt from income tax. Any amount above this threshold is subject to income tax and, in some cases, National Insurance contributions.

stem: When Income Tax operates on a self-assessment basis, what is the normal deadline for filing the tax return online?
explanation: The deadline for filing a self-assessment tax return online is 31 January following the end of the tax year. For example, for the tax year ending 5 April, the online return must be filed by the following 31 January. The 31 October deadline applies to paper returns.

stem: Andrew is an equity partner and also a higher-rate taxpayer. If he contributes to a personal pension, how will he receive tax relief?
options: He will pay gross and claim all the tax relief through his annual tax return.
He will pay net of starting-rate tax and claim the additional tax relief through his annual tax return.
He will pay net of basic-rate tax and claim the additional tax relief through his annual tax return.
He will pay net of higher-rate tax.
explanation: Personal pension contributions are made net of basic-rate tax under the relief at source method. The pension provider reclaims the basic-rate tax from HMRC. As a higher-rate taxpayer, Andrew must claim the additional higher-rate tax relief through his self-assessment tax return.

stem: Upon a claim being accepted under a group life scheme, to whom is the benefit contractually payable to from the insurer?
options: The beneficiary nominated by the member.
explanation: Under a group life scheme, the insurance contract is between the insurer and the scheme trustees (or the employer as policyholder). On a valid claim, the insurer pays the benefit to the scheme trustees, who then exercise their discretion to distribute it to the appropriate beneficiaries.

stem: A new employee has just joined a registered pension scheme. What is the maximum lump-sum death-in-service benefit that can be paid free of tax, if any?
options: The lump sum and death benefit allowance.
explanation: For registered pension schemes, the maximum lump-sum death benefit that can be paid tax-free is limited to the lump sum and death benefit allowance (LSDBA). This replaced the previous lifetime allowance framework and sets the cap on tax-free lump sum death benefits.

stem: A group life dependant's pension scheme is linked to an occupational pension scheme established in 2007. What annual rate of Limited Price Indexation is required, if any?
options: An annual rate is not required.
2.5% or the Consumer Price Index if less.
3.0% or the Consumer Price Index if less.
5.0% or the Consumer Price Index if less.
explanation: For occupational pension schemes established on or after 6 April 2005, Limited Price Indexation (LPI) requires annual increases of the lesser of 2.5% or the increase in the Consumer Price Index. Schemes established before that date had higher LPI caps of 5% or 3% depending on when benefits were accrued.

stem: Under employment law, an individual on a one-year fixed-term contract must be treated as though he is a permanent employee if his contract is rolled over for a period which exceeds what threshold number of consecutive years?
explanation: Under the Fixed-term Employees (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2002, a fixed-term employee who has been continuously employed for four or more years on successive fixed-term contracts is deemed to be a permanent employee, unless the employer can objectively justify continued use of fixed-term contracts.

stem: Under data protection legislation, for what maximum period is an insurer permitted to retain an individual's medical records?
options: For three years after cover for the individual ceases.
For seven years after the information was first obtained.
For as long as is necessary for the purpose it was obtained.
Until the individual specifically requests its removal or destruction.
explanation: Under data protection legislation (UK GDPR), personal data including medical records should be kept for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which it was obtained. There is no fixed statutory retention period; rather, the insurer must justify retention based on the original purpose of processing.

stem: Under a group income protection scheme, the normal restriction on third party rights is limited to allow an employee to
options: be told the premium level applicable to him for recording benefits-in-kind.
participate in decisions relating to the amendment or termination of the scheme.
query an insurer's decision to restrict the level or scope of insured benefit in excess of the free cover limit.
refer claims disputes to the Financial Ombudsman Service without prior reference to the employer.
explanation: Although the employee is not a party to the group insurance contract, the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 provisions are typically limited in group schemes. However, a key exception is preserved: employees retain the right to refer claims disputes directly to the Financial Ombudsman Service without needing prior reference to or permission from their employer.

stem: Steven has flexibly accessed a money purchase arrangement. What is his current money purchase annual allowance?
explanation: When an individual flexibly accesses their money purchase pension benefits (e.g., through flexi-access drawdown or an uncrystallised funds pension lump sum), their money purchase annual allowance is reduced to £10,000. This limit was increased from £4,000 to £10,000 from April 2023.

stem: What does the Financial Conduct Authority require from all authorised firms?
options: A minimum capital adequacy requirement of £100,000.
explanation: The FCA's Consumer Duty (introduced July 2023) applies to all authorised firms and requires them to act to deliver good outcomes for retail customers. It sets higher standards of consumer protection across financial services. The other options are not universal requirements for all authorised firms.

stem: To comply with the Financial Conduct Authority's rules, an intermediary must first provide details of how to make a complaint to a company that is purchasing critical illness cover at the time of issuing the
explanation: The Initial Disclosure Document (IDD) is the first formal document presented to a client and must include complaints procedures. It is provided at the outset of the relationship, before any quotation or policy document, making it the earliest point at which complaint details are disclosed.

stem: A firm of appointed representatives operates in the group risk market. In accordance with the Financial Conduct Authority's rules, the authorised person responsible for the actions of the firm is normally described as
explanation: Under FCA rules, an appointed representative acts on behalf of an authorised firm known as the 'principal.' The principal takes regulatory responsibility for the appointed representative's activities and must ensure the firm complies with FCA rules.

stem: A claimant under a group income protection scheme wishes to complain about the decision reached by the insurer. What time limit applies, if any, to referring the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service?
explanation: A complainant must refer their complaint to the Financial Ombudsman Service within 6 months of receiving the insurer's final response letter. After this period, the FOS will generally not consider the complaint unless there are exceptional circumstances.

stem: The Association of British Insurers' Guide to Minimum Standards for Critical Illness Insurance recommends that insurers use a common format, common generic terms and
options: a minimum of at least 7 conditions.
a survival period of not more than 28 days.
explanation: The ABI Guide to Minimum Standards recommends that insurers use a common format, common generic terms, and model wordings for critical illness definitions. This promotes consistency and transparency across the industry, making it easier for customers to compare policies.

stem: How often each year must a group life insurer provide the Financial Conduct Authority with a formal report, in the prescribed format, covering complaints?
explanation: FCA rules require authorised firms, including group life insurers, to submit formal complaints reports twice a year (i.e., every six months) in the prescribed format. This biannual reporting enables the FCA to monitor complaint trends and identify potential consumer harm.

stem: Firms in the financial adviser sector regularly collect and record information on their customer's profile and the advice given. What is the most likely reason for this?
options: An increased capital adequacy monitoring requirement.
A stricter money laundering record keeping requirement.
explanation: The Consumer Duty requires firms to act to deliver good outcomes for customers. Collecting detailed customer profile information and documenting advice given is essential to demonstrating that the firm has acted in the customer's best interests and delivered suitable recommendations.

stem: The normal retirement age under a company's defined benefit pension scheme is 60 years old, and is reassured under a group life scheme reflecting this. Consequently, what will typically happen to a member's insurance cover on reaching this age?
options: It will automatically be extended to age 65.
It will automatically be extended to the greater of State Pension age or age 65.
explanation: Group life cover linked to a pension scheme typically ceases when the member reaches the scheme's normal retirement age. Since the group life scheme reflects the pension scheme's retirement age of 60, the member's cover will cease immediately upon reaching age 60.

stem: A lump-sum death claim has been made under a registered group scheme. The deceased had died whilst flying a plane in his leisure time. Medical underwriting had recently been obtained for benefits in excess of the free cover level, but the requested information regarding a private pilot's licence was NOT disclosed. If the insurer has a policy of declining aviation risks, what would it do?
options: Accept the claim for the full amount as the policy is written in the name of the trustees.
Accept the claim up to the scheme's free cover limit.
Refuse the entire claim and void the policy.
Refuse the entire claim on the basis of the deceased's non-disclosure.
explanation: The non-disclosure only relates to the individual medical underwriting that was required for benefits above the free cover limit. The insurer would therefore pay the claim up to the free cover limit (which did not require underwriting) but decline the excess amount where the non-disclosure occurred.

stem: A large company is arranging a group income protection scheme to cover its 600 staff. At what point(s) can eligible new entrants be permitted to join the scheme?
options: At the annual review date only.
At the annual review date or at monthly intervals only.
At the annual review date, or lifestyle events only.
At whatever frequency the employer chooses.
explanation: For group income protection schemes, the employer has flexibility to determine when new entrants can join. There is no regulatory restriction on the frequency; the employer can allow new joiners at any frequency they choose, whether monthly, quarterly, or on specific dates.

stem: Under which type of main group benefit are pre-existing condition exclusions most often used as an across-the-board restriction covering all members?
explanation: Group critical illness schemes most commonly apply blanket pre-existing condition exclusions across all members. This is because critical illness claims are particularly susceptible to anti-selection, and conditions such as cancer may have been diagnosed before the cover commenced. Group life and income protection schemes less commonly use across-the-board pre-existing condition exclusions.

stem: To reduce the risk of anti-selection, the point at which a member can increase her benefit basis under a flexible benefit scheme is often restricted to the annual review date and a short period immediately after the
options: awarding of an across-the-board pay rise.
awarding of an individual merit rise.
explanation: To limit anti-selection risk, flexible benefit schemes typically restrict the opportunity to increase cover to the annual review date or shortly after a qualifying life event (such as marriage, birth of a child, or taking on a mortgage). This prevents members from increasing cover only when they anticipate a claim.

stem: Frank is in receipt of an income protection benefit of £20,000 per annum. The annual escalation rate of the benefit is 3%. What would Frank's income protection benefit be in two years' time?
explanation: The escalation is applied on a compound basis. After year 1: £20,000 x 1.03 = £20,600. After year 2: £20,600 x 1.03 = £21,218. The answer of £21,200 would be the result of simple (non-compound) escalation, which is incorrect.

stem: What tax(es), if any, will a dependant NOT be liable to pay on receipt of dependants pension that the original scheme member will have been liable to?
options: Both Income Tax and National Insurance.
explanation: A dependant's pension is subject to Income Tax, just as the original member's income protection benefit was. However, National Insurance contributions are not payable on a dependant's pension because the dependant is not in employment. The original member would have been liable for NICs on their earned income.

stem: Group critical illness cover provides a
options: lump-sum payment for medical fees when the employee suffers an accident.
lump-sum payment when the employee dies.
lump-sum payment upon diagnosis of one of a defined list of serious conditions.
regular payment when the employee becomes incapacitated to work.
explanation: Group critical illness insurance pays a one-off lump sum upon the diagnosis of a specified serious condition from a defined list (e.g., cancer, heart attack, stroke). It is not a death benefit (that is group life), not for accidents specifically, and does not pay regular income (that is group income protection).

stem: In order to keep the costs of a group income protection scheme to an absolute minimum, what general approach is likely to be recommended for the deferred period?
explanation: A longer deferred period means the insurer does not start paying benefits until a longer period of incapacity has elapsed, which significantly reduces the premium. During the deferred period, the employer typically continues to pay the employee's salary, so a longer deferral shifts more of the short-term sickness cost to the employer but reduces the insurance premium.

stem: A company which operates a group death-in-service scheme has advised their financial adviser that the company is relocating from a rural postcode to an inner city location. When should the adviser inform the insurer?
options: As soon as he is advised of the relocation.
Only when all members of the scheme have relocated.
explanation: A change in the employer's location from rural to inner city could be a material change affecting the risk profile (e.g., due to event risk or concentration of lives). The adviser has a duty to inform the insurer of material changes as soon as they become aware of them, not wait until the next renewal.

stem: Barbara is a financial adviser. She is advising her client to accept a quotation for a new death-in-service scheme with a higher premium because it offers a higher free cover limit. Why is Barbara likely to do this?
options: Any medical examinations required will be free of charge.
The higher premium will cross-subsidise other group schemes that Barbara places with the insurer.
It may reduce the need for any medical evidence for members.
It will pay her the highest level of commission.
explanation: A higher free cover limit means that more members will have benefits within the limit and therefore will not need to provide individual medical evidence. This reduces the administrative burden, speeds up the process, and avoids the risk that members may be declined or loaded following underwriting.

stem: An employee is NOT actively at work upon commencement of a new group income protection scheme. The intermediary should therefore notify the employer that the employee's benefits under the new scheme will be
options: nil until conditions set by the insurer are met.
operating on a purely temporary basis.
restricted to the free cover level.
underwritten at the next annual review date.
explanation: Group income protection schemes typically include an 'actively at work' requirement at commencement. If an employee is not actively at work when the scheme starts, they will have no cover until they satisfy the insurer's conditions (usually returning to full-time active work for a specified period).

stem: A new registered group life scheme has a proposed benefit basis of four times salary for senior employees and two times salary for all other employees. The intermediary has been requested to clarify the term 'senior' using objectively verifiable criteria. What are the two main reasons for this?
options: To avoid age discrimination and to manage anti-selection risk.
To avoid age discrimination and to meet HM Revenue & Customs conditions.
To ensure contract certainty and to manage anti-selection risk.
To ensure contract certainty and to meet HM Revenue & Customs conditions.
explanation: Objectively defining 'senior' is needed for two reasons: first, to ensure contract certainty so the insurer and employer are clear about who qualifies for each benefit level; and second, to manage anti-selection risk by preventing individuals from being selectively moved into higher benefit categories. HMRC does not require specific definitions of employee categories for registered schemes.

stem: Under the Insurance: Conduct of Business sourcebook (ICOBS), an Initial Disclosure Document presented to a commercial client at the correct time is designed to satisfy status disclosure, scope of service and
explanation: The Initial Disclosure Document (IDD) under ICOBS must cover three key areas: status disclosure (who the intermediary is and their regulatory status), scope of service (what services they provide), and remuneration (how they are paid, e.g., commission or fees). Product information is provided separately.

stem: When, if ever, should an independent financial adviser inform a client that he is potentially dealing with an insurance company which is outside of the adviser's own list of approved group risk insurers?
options: Immediately on receipt of the quotation.
The client does not need to be informed.
explanation: An independent financial adviser should inform the client during the recommendation process if a recommended insurer is outside their normal approved list. At this stage the adviser is making a specific recommendation, and the client needs to understand any limitations in the adviser's due diligence on that insurer.

stem: In what circumstances, if any, could the initial premium on a new group life scheme be paid to the insurer prior to registering the scheme with HM Revenue & Customs to ensure a tax advantage is obtained?
options: Only if the scheme solely provides lump-sum benefits.
Only if the scheme solely provides dependant's pensions.
explanation: A group life scheme must be registered with HMRC before premiums are paid in order for the employer to obtain tax relief on the premiums. Paying premiums before registration means the tax advantages of a registered scheme would not apply, so this should not be done in any circumstances.

stem: A small company has had no claims under its income protection scheme which insures 66% of salary, with a deferred period of 26 weeks, 3% escalation and payment to the greater of 65 or State Pension age. They are looking to save money, which change would be likely to give the largest premium reduction?
options: Reduce the benefit basis to 50% of salary.
Reduce the deferred period to 13 weeks.
Reduce the escalation to the lesser of 2.5% or the Retail Prices Index.
Reduce the payment period to 2 years.
explanation: Reducing the maximum payment period from potentially decades (to age 65 or SPA) to just 2 years would produce the largest premium saving because it dramatically reduces the insurer's potential liability on each claim. Reducing the benefit basis or escalation rate would have a smaller impact, and reducing the deferred period to 13 weeks would actually increase premiums.

stem: An insurer has received group life quotation requests for two unconnected companies. Due to event limit restrictions, the insurer is only willing to quote for one of them. What differentiating factor between the two companies is most likely to have triggered the refusal to quote?
explanation: Event limits restrict the insurer's maximum exposure to a single catastrophic event (such as a natural disaster or terrorist attack). Geographical location is the key factor because if both companies are in the same area, covering both would concentrate the insurer's risk. Quoting for only one reduces the potential loss from a single event affecting that location.

stem: A quotation has been accepted on a single premium basis and the scheme commencement date is 1 June 2025. On what date is the underlying premium rate table most likely to expire?
explanation: For schemes costed on a single premium basis, the underlying rate table (not the premium itself) is typically guaranteed for two years from commencement. The scheme commenced on 1 June 2025, so the rate table would most likely expire on 31 May 2027, after which the insurer may revise the rates.

stem: When a prospective member joins a group scheme as a discretionary entrant, what benefits will typically be subject to underwriting, if any?
options: The benefit in excess of the free cover limit only.
The level of salary in excess of the scheme's average only.
explanation: A discretionary entrant is someone who does not automatically qualify for scheme membership and is included at the employer's or trustees' discretion. Because their inclusion is not part of the standard eligibility criteria, the insurer will typically require underwriting of all their benefits, not just those above the free cover limit.

stem: Tom is a member of a group life scheme which is costed on a single premium basis with premiums paid annually. If he left employment exactly eight months into the scheme year, what premium refund, if any, will usually be granted?
options: A premium refund will not be granted.
One third of the individual premium.
explanation: Under a single premium basis, when a member leaves during the scheme year, a pro-rata refund is typically given for the unexpired period. Tom left after 8 months with 4 months remaining out of 12, so the refund would be 4/12 = one third of the individual premium.

stem: A group life scheme with single premium costing was quoted for in January. It is placed with an insurer on 1 April. Although the benefit basis, membership and salaries are unchanged, no members exceed the free cover limit and the quotation guarantee period has NOT expired, the first account shows the cost has increased. What is the most likely reason for this?
options: The insurer has altered its underlying rates.
One or more members have not satisfied the actively at work conditions.
One or more members have passed their birthday.
explanation: With single premium costing, the premium is calculated using age-related rates for each individual member. Between the quotation date in January and the placement on 1 April, some members will have had birthdays, moving them into higher age bands. This increases the total premium even though all other factors remain unchanged.

stem: An insurer issued a group life quotation to a financial adviser on 1 April. Until what date will this quotation normally be guaranteed?
explanation: Group life quotations are normally guaranteed for a period of three months (90 days) from the date of issue. A quotation issued on 1 April would therefore be guaranteed until 1 July.

stem: Coinsurance is when
options: part of the risk is passed by an insurer to a reinsurer.
part of the risk is passed by a reinsurer to another reinsurer.
the whole risk is split between more than one insurer.
the whole risk is split into a number of policies with one insurer.
explanation: Coinsurance is the arrangement where the whole risk is shared (split) between two or more insurers, each taking a proportion of the risk and premium. This differs from reinsurance, where an insurer passes part of an accepted risk to a reinsurer, and from retrocession, where a reinsurer passes risk to another reinsurer.

stem: A retrocession agreement in respect of a group life scheme is arranged directly between which parties?
options: The scheme trustees and the reinsurer.
explanation: Retrocession is the process by which a reinsurer passes on some of the risk it has accepted to another reinsurer. Therefore, a retrocession agreement is arranged directly between one reinsurer and another reinsurer. This differs from reinsurance, which is between an insurer and a reinsurer.
